source.title: Serotonin Selectively Modulates Reward Value in Human Decision-Making

confidence_rationale: Human tryptophan depletion study (reducing 5-HT) selectively impaired the neural and behavioral representation of reward outcome value in a concurrent reward-and-pain task, while leaving punishment processing relatively intact. This is direct causal evidence (via tryptophan depletion) in humans that 5-HT specifically supports the *appetitive* side of valuation -- when 5-HT is reduced, reward value representations in vmPFC degrade. This maps onto MECH-186's claim that tonic 5-HT maintains the VALENCE_WANTING floor (the benefit-side terrain). The effect was computationally dissociable from choice perseveration effects, suggesting it is specifically about value representation rather than general motivation or motor output. Confidence moderate: this is a human study using a well-validated depletion method, but the paradigm mixes reward and pain simultaneously and the floor-maintenance interpretation requires inference beyond what the paper claims.
failure_signatures: The depletion procedure reduces 5-HT globally -- it cannot dissociate tonic from phasic 5-HT contributions or isolate the dorsal raphe floor-maintenance mechanism specifically
The task involves simultaneous monetary reward and physical pain; the interaction structure may not cleanly generalize to sustained harm-dominant environments
Effect was on reward value representation (vmPFC cue-value activity), not on the terrain architecture per se -- the mapping to VALENCE_WANTING entries requires REE-side inference

mapping.source_claim_statement: Acute tryptophan depletion (lowering central 5-HT) selectively impaired behavioral and neural representations of reward outcome value -- reducing vmPFC cue-value responses and dorsolateral putamen prediction-error signals for rewards, while leaving punishment processing relatively intact.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-186 claims that tonic 5-HT maintains the VALENCE_WANTING floor in the residue field -- without it, benefit terrain goes flat under harm-dominant conditions. Seymour et al. show the causal flip side: when 5-HT is reduced experimentally, reward value representations selectively degrade in the brain circuits responsible for tracking benefit value (vmPFC, dorsolateral putamen). The selectivity for the reward side -- while punishment processing is relatively preserved -- is consistent with MECH-186's claim that 5-HT specifically maintains the benefit gradient, not harm sensitivity. This is the only existing human causal evidence that 5-HT level changes bidirectionally track the quality of benefit terrain representation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Depletion reduces 5-HT globally over hours; MECH-186 posits a specifically tonic, slow-accumulating signal. The vmPFC effect could reflect changed phasic 5-HT as much as tonic baseline. The floor-maintenance story requires that the tonic component be the critical one -- this paper cannot isolate it.
mapping.source_context: Human double-blind crossover fMRI study, n=21, acute dietary tryptophan depletion, novel concurrent reward-and-pain choice task, no psychiatric history.

confidence_components.notes: Journal of Neuroscience, strong design with fMRI + behavior + computational model, human data. Mapping fidelity moderate -- the depletion-to-floor-maintenance link requires a further mechanistic step. Transfer risk relatively low since this is human data directly.
